title: Single Qubit Gates

theory.introduction: Quantum gates are operations that change the state of a qubit. Just like classical logic gates (AND, NOT) manipulate bits, quantum gates manipulate qubits. However, unlike classical gates, quantum gates are strictly reversible and operate linearly through complex matrices, preserving the fundamental quantum information embedded within.
theory.start_from_zero: Think of a qubit as a coordinate point traversing the surface of a 3D globe (the Bloch Sphere). Quantum gates act as physical transformations that rotate this point along different axes. Instead of harshly snapping between 0 and 1, gates glide the state in a smooth, continuous mathematical space.
theory.why_it_matters: They are the fundamental atomic units of all quantum computation. Masterfully sequencing these basic X, Y, Z, and H rotations is what ultimately generates superposition, structural interference, and entanglement—the core phenomena responsible for quantum speedup.
theory.core_idea: A quantum gate is a mathematical operation represented by a matrix. When applied, it multiplies with the qubit state vector and produces a new quantum state.
step_by_step_understanding: Step 2: Represent it as a vector
Step 5: Measure to get a classical result
key_points: Quantum gates operate using matrix multiplication
Some gates affect probability, others affect phase
Gates can be visualized as rotations on the Bloch sphere

bloch_sphere_connection.description: Quantum gates can be understood as rotations of a qubit on the Bloch Sphere.
examples: X gate rotates the qubit around the X-axis
Y gate rotates around the Y-axis
Z gate rotates around the Z-axis
Hadamard moves the qubit from pole to equator
bloch_sphere_connection.importance: This visualization helps understand how gates transform quantum states continuously.

gates.name: Pauli-X Gate (NOT Gate)
gates.description: Flips the qubit state, similar to a classical NOT gate.
gates.intuition: Like flipping a coin from heads to tails.
gates.bloch_effect: Rotates the qubit 180° around the X-axis
gates.deep_note: Unlike classical NOT, this works even on superpositions.

gates.name: Pauli-Z Gate
gates.description: Changes the phase of the |1⟩ component but leaves |0⟩ unchanged.
gates.intuition: It changes the 'sign' of the state without affecting probabilities.
gates.bloch_effect: Rotates the qubit 180° around the Z-axis
gates.deep_note: Phase changes are invisible in measurement but crucial in interference.

gates.name: Pauli-Y Gate
gates.description: Applies both bit flip and phase change together.
gates.intuition: A combination of X and Z effects with a complex phase.
gates.bloch_effect: Rotates the qubit 180° around the Y-axis
gates.deep_note: Introduces complex phase (i), which is important in quantum interference.

gates.name: Hadamard Gate
gates.description: Creates superposition by transforming basis states into equal combinations.
gates.intuition: Splits the qubit into equal possibilities.
gates.bloch_effect: Moves the qubit from pole to equator
gates.deep_note: This gate is essential for creating quantum parallelism.

examples.title: H followed by X
examples.insight: Order of gates matters, but here the state remains symmetric.

examples.title: H followed by Z
examples.insight: Probabilities stay same, but phase changes.

common_mistakes: Thinking Z gate changes measurement outcome
Assuming quantum gates behave like classical logic gates
Forgetting that order of gates matters
Not understanding that gates act on amplitudes, not probabilities directly

try_this: Apply H then measure multiple times
Apply Z after H and observe phase change
Try X before H and compare results

quiz.question: Which gate flips a qubit?
quiz.explanation: X gate flips |0⟩ to |1⟩ and vice versa.

quiz.question: Which gate changes phase only?
quiz.explanation: Z gate changes phase without affecting probability.

quiz.question: What does the Hadamard gate do?
